title: Chapter 35: The Leak
content: The phone wakes me.

Not my phone — his. Rowan's. It's six in the morning and the room is still dark, the kind of dark that only exists fourteen stories above the street, and his phone is lighting up the way a city lights up during an air raid. Steady. Relentless. Wrong.

I reach for mine before I'm fully awake. Muscle memory. The screen brightness hits my pupils like a blade and I squint against it, and the notifications are a wall — Suki, Sable, Suki again, a number I don't recognize, another number I don't recognize, Suki a third time. The previews scroll past my dilated eyes in fragments: *oh my god* and *are you awake* and *please tell me you're seeing this* and then, from Suki, the link:
**OMG READ THIS NOW**
I tap it.
The article loads. The header image fills my screen and the floor drops out from under me.

It's my face. The 1943 photograph — the one from the Venice archive, the one I thought was buried in a file cabinet that no one cared enough to open. My face, black and white, unsmiling, wearing clothes I designed myself in a decade when women didn't design anything. The same cheekbones. The same mouth. The same eyes that are staring back at me right now from the screen of a phone in a penthouse in Manhattan, eighty-three years later, unchanged.
The headline sits beneath it like a guillotine blade:
**The Ashford Foundation's Impossible Designer: Questions of Identity, Authenticity, and Anomaly**
*By Dimitri Voss*
I read it. I read all of it. Every surgically precise sentence.

It is not tabloid work. That's what makes it devastating. A tabloid would have called me a vampire. A freak. A hoax. Dimitri doesn't do any of that. He doesn't need to. He just lays out the evidence with the cold efficiency of a coroner's report and lets the reader arrive at the horror on their own.

The photographs — six of them, spanning two centuries. The 1943 portrait. A painting from 1889 that Dimitri sourced from a private collection in Vienna; the sitter is catalogued as *Unknown Woman, Attributed to Circle of Klimt*, and she has my jaw, my brow, my hands resting in her lap the way I always rest my hands when I'm sitting for a portrait because I've been sitting for portraits since portraits were the only way to record a face. A daguerreotype from 1812. A sketch from 1743 — not just any sketch, the letter, the Venice letter I wrote to no one in particular because I needed to put words on paper or I was going to drown in the canal of my own mind, and Dimitri found it. He had it analyzed by a handwriting expert — *Dr. Elaine Marchetti, certified forensic document examiner* — who confirms that the script matches my current handwriting with a statistical probability that makes coincidence laughable.

The medical records. Or rather, the gaps in them. Dimitri's article notes that my documented medical history begins in 2019 and is, quote, "curiously devoid of any pediatric records, immunization history, or evidence of prior care despite the subject's apparent age of thirty-one." He doesn't say I'm immortal. He says the records don't add up.
He says I'm an *anomaly*.

The word appears four times in the article. Anomaly. Not fraud. Not liar. Not charlatan. Something worse. Something that can't be debunked because it hasn't been claimed. He's not accusing me of pretending to be hundreds of years old — he's presenting evidence that I might actually *be* something that shouldn't exist, and asking the Ashford Foundation to explain why they've built an entire initiative around someone whose identity cannot be verified by any conventional metric.

The comments section loads the way a wound opens — slowly, then all at once. Fourteen thousand comments in eight hours. I scroll. BELIEVER: *Her work is proof. Those garments are not reproductions. I've studied 18th-century textile construction for twenty years and what she does is IMPOSSIBLE without firsthand knowledge.* SKEPTIC: *This is obviously an elaborate ARG or a PR stunt for the collection.* TRANSPHOBE: *He's a man in a dress who needs mental health intervention.* Someone has already made a subreddit. Someone is calling me a prophet. Someone is calling me a monster. Someone is doxxing the Ashford Foundation's board members. Someone is suggesting I be detained and studied.
I put the phone down on the sheets.

My hands are shaking. Not trembling — shaking, the way a building shakes before it collapses. The hum is not building. The hum is *here*. It's a roar inside my body, not a sound but a full-body seizure of frequency, vibrating through my bones, my marrow, the spaces between my cells. My skin is shimmering. I can see it in the pre-dawn dark — the faint iridescent ripple across my forearms, the way light bends wrong around my knuckles. The shift isn't approaching. I'm already *in* it.

I feel my jaw trying to move. The bones in my face pressing outward, the maxilla shifting against the temporal, a pressure like a hand slowly squeezing. I grip the sheets in both fists. The cotton bunches and twists. The texture is too much — every thread a cable, every fiber a rope burning against my skin. I am overloading. Every sensation is a nail being driven into my nervous system and the hum is the hammer.

Across the room, Rowan is on the phone. He's been on the phone since before I woke — I realize this now, hearing the cadence of his voice in the other room, the controlled fury of it. He's talking to his legal team. Then his PR team. Then — and this is the one that makes my stomach drop — his mother. His voice is a general's voice. Commands. Directives. *No comment until we've reviewed the full text. Issue the cease-and-desist on the photographs — we hold the copyright on the 1943 image through the Foundation archive. Flag the handwriting analysis as inadmissible — chain of custody on the document hasn't been established.* He is fighting a war on three fronts and he sounds like he was built for exactly this.

He comes to the doorway. His eyes find mine. And for one second — one fraction of a second — his face goes soft. The general becomes the man who held me last night, who whispered against my shoulder, who said *I see you* like it was the truest thing anyone had ever said. His jaw tightens. The softness is gone. He turns back to the phone and his voice is steel again.

I get out of bed. I'm wearing his shirt — the white one, the one that smells like cedar and lanolin and him, the one I stole from the chair at three in the morning because I couldn't sleep and the fabric against my skin was the only thing that quieted the hum. It doesn't quiet the hum now. Nothing quiets the hum now.

I walk to the window. The city is out there, sixteen million windows, and every single one of them is a screen. I press my forehead against the glass. The cool surface bites my skin — the morning air seeping through the pane, carrying the smell of exhaust and rain-soaked concrete and the strange metallic ozone of a city that never stops running. I breathe it in and it tastes like survival, which is to say it tastes like nothing good.
My phone lights up again. Not texts this time. Calls.

The first one is a New York Times reporter. I decline. The second is a podcast — *Supernatural Stories: Real or Real Fake?* I decline. The third is a documentary filmmaker. The fourth is a number with a Virginia area code and a voicemail that begins, *Ms. Ashford, my name is Dr. Raymond Liu, I'm with the National Institutes of Health, Office of Rare Diseases Research, and we'd like to discuss—* I delete it.

The calls don't stop. They multiply like cells dividing. Producers. Professors. A man who claims to be a government research agent and refuses to specify which government. A woman who says she's my great-great-granddaughter and she has the DNA test to prove it. Every notification is a small explosion against the inside of my skull and the hum swells with each one, taking up more space, pressing against the walls of my body like water against a dam.
I watch the city. I think about Seville.

1609. The Inquisition. Different tools, same instinct. They didn't have the Internet. They had doors in the middle of the night and rooms with no windows and questions that had no right answer. They had fire. They had the smell of it — woodsmoke and rendered fat and the sickening sweet of burning hair — and they had the crowds who came to watch, because there have always been crowds who come to watch, people who need to see the thing that doesn't fit shoved into a shape that does.
The tools have changed. The instinct hasn't.

The crowd used to gather in a plaza. Now it gathers in a comments section. The plaza had edges; you could leave. The comments section is infinite. It replicates. It screenshots. It archives. In Seville, I could disappear — change my name, change my face, walk to another city and start again. The shift was liberation then. The shift was the door out of the room with no windows.

But you can't disappear from the Internet. You can shift every bone in your body and they'll still have the photographs. They'll still have the handwriting analysis. They'll still have fourteen thousand comments and a subreddit and someone claiming to be my great-great-granddaughter and the whole sprawling architecture of curiosity and contempt that Dimitri built with one surgical article in the dark hours of the morning.
I taste it — the sour tang of last night's wine and sleep, the acid fear rising from my stomach, coating my tongue. I swallow it down. I've swallowed worse.
Rowan's voice stops. The silence is louder than the phone calls.

He appears behind me. I don't turn around — I can feel him, the way I always feel him, the gravitational pull of his body in space, the warmth of him approaching across the cold floor. His hands find mine. My hands are still shaking. His aren't.

"Listen to me." His voice is low, steady, the voice he uses when he's anchoring something that wants to fly apart. "This is not Seville. This is not an Inquisition. This is the twenty-first century and we have resources they didn't."
I keep my forehead against the glass. The city breathes below us — traffic and sirens and the hum of millions of people waking up to find my face on their screens.
"They had fire," I say. "These people have the Internet. I don't know which is worse."

He turns me around. Gently but firmly, the way you turn something fragile that might shatter if you grip too hard or let go too fast. His eyes are red-rimmed — he hasn't slept — but they're clear. Focused. He is a man who has been fighting since three in the morning and he hasn't lost yet.

"Fire destroys," he says. "The Internet amplifies. And right now, it's amplifying your work. The collection. People are seeing the clothes alongside the claims and they're asking questions, yes, but they're also saying the work is genius. The story is complicated." He squeezes my hands. "Complicated is manageable."

I want to believe him. I want to crawl inside his certainty and live there, in the fortress he's building out of legal briefs and PR strategies and the sheer force of his refusal to let me be consumed. Complicated is manageable. It sounds like something that could be true. It sounds like something a man says when the alternative is admitting that he can't protect you from what's coming.

Because what's coming isn't a news cycle. What's coming isn't a legal battle or a PR crisis or a comments-section war. What's coming is inside me, and it doesn't care about cease-and-desist orders or copyright claims or the twenty-first century's resources.
The hum doesn't agree with him. The hum is saying: it's time.

I can feel every cell in my body preparing. The way a storm prepares — not thinking, not choosing, just accumulating pressure until the discharge becomes inevitable. My jaw is shifting. My cheekbones are pressing against my skin like they're trying to push through. The shimmer is constant now, a faint ripple in the air around me, the way heat distorts the horizon in summer. Rowan can't see it in the dim room — or maybe he can, maybe that's why his grip tightens on my hands, maybe he feels the vibration running through my palms and into his.

The shift is coming. Not because I want it. Not because the calendar says so or the moon is right or any of the hundred small triggers I've learned to read over four hundred years. It's coming because the emotional overload has exceeded my body's capacity to contain it. Because the article and the comments and the phone calls and the exposure and the fear and the memory of Seville and the way Rowan is holding my hands like he'll never let go — all of it is too much. The container is cracking. The change is forcing its way through the fracture.
Not in weeks. Not in days.
Hours. Maybe less.

And I'm standing in a penthouse in a city that is currently debating my existence on every screen in the world, and the only person who knows what's about to happen to me is looking at me like complicated is manageable, and the hum is roaring through my bones like a river through a broken dam, and there is nowhere to go, nowhere to hide, no doorway out of this room — just the glass and the city and the shift rushing toward me like a train in a tunnel, and I can hear it coming, I can feel it in every cell, and it will not be stopped.
